name: research-to-answer
description: Answer a question that needs several web sources: fan out searches over independent angles, synthesize one source-cited answer, and check it is grounded.

input.description: a question that needs gathering and weighing several sources

output.description: a source-cited answer that passed a grounding check

nodes.title: Plan research angles
nodes.prompt: Break the question into 3-6 independent research angles. Write each angle as a SELF-CONTAINED search query that names the specific subject of the question, so it stands on its own without the original question. Deliver the queries as the items list of your structured output, e.g. "health benefits of X", "risks and side effects of X".

items.description: Self-contained search queries, one per research angle.

nodes.title: Search the web
nodes.prompt: Investigate the self-contained research query you were given. Make AT MOST 2-3 web searches and read AT MOST 3-4 sources total, then STOP — do not re-fetch a URL you already read or keep hunting for more. Return concise findings, each claim followed by its source (a URL or title). Cite specifically; never invent a source or summarize vaguely. If sources are thin, say so rather than over-searching.

nodes.title: Synthesize the answer
nodes.prompt: Using the gathered findings, write one coherent, well-structured answer to the original question. Keep every non-trivial claim tied to a source from the findings. Where the findings are thin, say so rather than guessing.

nodes.title: Verify grounding
nodes.prompt:
Judge whether the answer is grounded in the gathered findings, claim by claim — not all-or-nothing.

For each SUBSTANTIVE factual claim in the answer, check whether a gathered finding supports it, and apply these tolerance rules:
- A brief summary or opening sentence that restates conclusions which ARE supported later in the body does NOT need its own citation — judge it by the body.
- Do not fail the whole answer for one or two minor unsupported details; judge by whether the BULK of the substantive claims are supported and whether the planned angles were covered.
- Hedges, general context and opinions are not factual claims and do not count.

Give a one-line reason, then end your reply with EXACTLY one of these labels on its own final line:
GROUNDED — the substantive claims are supported by the findings (a clean summary intro and one or two minor gaps are acceptable).
MISSING  — one or more SUBSTANTIVE claims have NO support in the findings, or a planned angle went unanswered. Name specifically which claims/angles are missing.
MISUSED  — a claim contradicts or overstates what its finding actually says; it should be rewritten from the existing findings.
